en.extract:
The ruddy cuckoo-dove (Macropygia emiliana) is a species of bird in the family Columbidae. It is a medium-sized, reddish brown cuckoo-dove, found in Brunei, Indonesia, and Malaysia. It is rated as a species of least concern on the International Union for Conservation of Nature Red List of Endangered Species.


Taxonomy
The ruddy cuckoo-dove was first described by the French biologist and ornithologist Charles Lucien Jules Laurent Bonaparte in 1854. It was split from the slender-billed cuckoo-dove in 2003. It has two remaining sub-species:

M. e. emiliana Bonaparte, 1854 - Java to western Lesser Sunda Islands
M. e. megala Siebers, 1929 - Kangean Islands
Five former subspecies of the ruddy cuckoo-dove were reassigned by the IOU in 2016:

M. e. cinnamomea Salvadori, 1892 - Enggano Island was split from the ruddy cuckoo-dove as the Enggano cuckoo-dove.
M. e. borneensis Robinson & Kloss, 1921 - northern Borneo is now provisionally considered a subspecies of the Philippine cuckoo-dove(Macropygia tenuirostris borneensis).
M. e. modiglianii Salvadori, 1887 - western Sumatran islands is now considered a subspecies of the Barusan cuckoo-dove.
M. e. elassa Oberholser, 1912 - Mentawai Islands is now considered a subspecies of the Barusan cuckoo-dove.
M. e. hypopercna Oberholser, 1912 - Simeulue Island is now considered a subspecies of the Barusan cuckoo-dove.


Description
The ruddy cuckoo-dove is a medium-sized, reddish brown dove, that measures 30 to 37 cm (12 to 15 in) in length. It has a uniformly ruddy head, and purplish brown, black-barred breast. The neck is pink iridescent. The tail is long and slender, and lacks any pale grey or white at the tip. The central tail coverts are uniformly ruddy, and lack any black barring. The juvenile resembles the female in appearance, but is more strongly barred.


Status and conservation
Since 1988, the ruddy cuckoo-dove has been rated as a species of least concern on the IUCN Red List of Endangered Species. This is because it has a very large range—more than 20,000 km2 (7,700 mi2)—and because although its population has been declining, the rate of decrease is considered to be less over 30% decline over ten years or three generations. In addition, although its population numbers have not been determined, it is thought to be above 10,000, which is above the criterion to warrant a vulnerable rating. It is described to be rarely found, but is common on the Borneo Island and on islands off the south and west coasts Sumatra. It is thought to face a population decline due to habitat destruction.


References


External links
 Media related to Macropygia emiliana at Wikimedia Commons
 Data related to Macropygia emiliana at Wikispecies

The '''ruddy cuckoo-dove''' ('''''Macropygia emiliana''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Columbidae]]. It is a medium-sized, reddish brown cuckoo-dove, found in [[Brunei]], [[Indonesia]], and [[Malaysia]]. It is rated as a species of [[least concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List of Endangered Species|International Union for Conservation of Nature Red List of Endangered Species]].<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" />

== Taxonomy ==
The ruddy cuckoo-dove was first described by the French biologist and ornithologist Charles Lucien Jules Laurent Bonaparte in 1854.<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> It was split from the [[slender-billed cuckoo-dove]] in 2003. It has two remaining sub-species:<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/pigeons/|title=Pigeons « IOC World Bird List|website=www.worldbirdnames.org|language=en-US|access-date=2017-09-24}}</ref>
* ''M. e. emiliana'' <small>[[Charles Lucien Bonaparte|Bonaparte]], 1854</small> - [[Java]] to western [[Lesser Sunda Islands]]
* ''M. e. megala'' <small>Siebers, 1929</small> - [[Kangean Islands]]
Five former subspecies of the ruddy cuckoo-dove were reassigned by the [[International Ornithologists' Union|IOU]] in 2016:
* ''M. e. cinnamomea'' <small>[[Tommaso Salvadori|Salvadori]], 1892</small> - [[Enggano Island]] was split from the ruddy cuckoo-dove as the [[Enggano cuckoo-dove]].
* ''M. e. borneensis'' <small>[[Herbert C. Robinson|Robinson]] & [[C. Boden Kloss|Kloss]], 1921</small> - northern [[Borneo]] is now provisionally considered a subspecies of the [[Philippine cuckoo-dove]](''Macropygia tenuirostris borneensis)''.
* ''M. e. modiglianii'' <small>[[Tommaso Salvadori|Salvadori]], 1887</small> - western [[Sumatra|Sumatran islands]] is now considered a subspecies of the [[Barusan cuckoo-dove]].
* ''M. e. elassa'' <small>[[Harry C. Oberholser|Oberholser]], 1912</small> - [[Mentawai Islands Regency|Mentawai Islands]] is now considered a subspecies of the [[Barusan cuckoo-dove]].
* ''M. e. hypopercna'' <small>[[Harry C. Oberholser|Oberholser]], 1912</small> - [[Simeulue|Simeulue Island]] is now considered a subspecies of the [[Barusan cuckoo-dove]].

== Description ==
The ruddy cuckoo-dove is a medium-sized,<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JMGkAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA267|title=Pigeons and Doves: A Guide to the Pigeons and Doves of the World|last=Gibbs|first=David|date=2010|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4081-3555-6|pages=267|language=en}}</ref> reddish brown dove, that measures {{Convert|30|to|37|cm|abbr=on}} in length.<ref name="hbw2">{{cite web|url=http://www.hbw.com/species/ruddy-cuckoo-dove-macropygia-emiliana|title=Ruddy Cuckoo-dove (''Macropygia emiliana'')|last1=Baptista|first1=L.F.|last2=Christie|first2=D A|year=2017|editor1-last=del Hoyo|editor1-first=Josep|editor2-last=Trail|editor2-first=P.W.|work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive|publisher=Lynx Edicions|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170712173107/http://www.hbw.com/species/ruddy-cuckoo-dove-macropygia-emiliana|archive-date=2017-07-12|access-date=2017-09-24|url-access=subscription |last3=Horblit|first3=H.M.|last4=Boesman|first4=P.|editor3-last=Sargatal|editor3-first=Jordi|editor4-last=Christie|editor4-first=David A|editor5-last=de Juana|editor5-first=Eduardo}}</ref> It has a uniformly ruddy head,<ref name=":0" /> and purplish brown, black-barred breast. The neck is pink iridescent.<ref name="hbw2" /> The tail is long and slender, and lacks any pale grey or white at the tip. The central tail coverts are uniformly ruddy, and lack any black barring.<ref name=":0" /> The juvenile resembles the female in appearance, but is more strongly barred.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=iLynCwAAQBAJ&pg=PA138|title=Birds of Borneo|last=Myers|first=Susan|date=2016|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4729-2445-2|pages=138|language=en}}</ref>

== Status and conservation ==
Since 1988, the ruddy cuckoo-dove has been rated as a species of [[least concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List of Endangered Species]]. This is because it has a very large range—more than 20,000 km<sup>2</sup> (7,700 mi<sup>2</sup>)—and because although its population has been declining, the rate of decrease is considered to be less over 30% decline over ten years or three generations. In addition, although its population numbers have not been determined, it is thought to be above 10,000, which is above the criterion to warrant a [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] rating. It is described to be rarely found, but is common on the [[Borneo Island]] and on islands off the south and west coasts [[Sumatra]]. It is thought to face a population decline due to [[habitat destruction]].<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" />
